content:
First, note that the $3\times3$ grid is a bipartite graph (checker‐board) with one colour‐class of size 5 and the other of size 4.  Since there are exactly five odd numbers $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$ and four even numbers $\{2,4,6,8\}$, the only way to have every edge join an odd to an even is to put all five odds on the 5‐cell class and all four evens on the 4‐cell class.  Hence  

 • choose a permutation of the five odd labels on the 5‐cell class: $5!$ ways,  
 • choose a permutation of the four even labels on the 4‐cell class: $4!$ ways.  

So there are in all  
$$5!\;\times\;4!\;=\;120\cdot24\;=\;2880$$  
Isthmian arrangements in total.  

Finally, we quotient out by the four rotations (the cyclic group of order 4).  By Burnside’s Lemma the only arrangement fixed by a nontrivial rotation would have to repeat labels on a 2‐ or 4‐cycle of cells, which is impossible since all labels $1,\dots,9$ are distinct.  Hence no nontrivial rotation fixes any arrangement, and the orbit‐count is simply  
$$\frac{2880}{4}\;=\;720\,. $$

Answer: \(\boxed{720}.\)

First, note that the $3\times3$ grid is a bipartite graph (checker‐board) with one colour‐class of size 5 and the other of size 4. Hence  

 • choose a permutation of the five odd labels on the 5‐cell class: $5!$ ways,  
 • choose a permutation of the four even labels on the 4‐cell class: $4!$ ways.  

So there are in all  
$$5!\;\times\;4!\;=\;120\cdot24\;=\;2880$$  
Isthmian arrangements in total.  

Finally, we quotient out by the four rotations (the cyclic group of order 4).  By Burnside’s Lemma the only arrangement fixed by a nontrivial rotation would have to repeat labels on a 2‐ or 4‐cycle of cells, which is impossible since all labels $1,\dots,9$ are distinct.  Hence no nontrivial rotation fixes any arrangement, and the orbit‐count is simply  
$$\frac{2880}{4}\;=\;720\,. $$

Answer: \(\boxed{720}.\)
